ontology.foodex2_description: Sugar and sugar products — powdered/icing sugar
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated icing sugar code; A000E (sugar and sugar products) is the closest defensible parent category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for icing sugar; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of icing sugar is approximately 1 tablespoon (about 8 g), used as a dusting or decorative topping on baked goods and desserts.

nutrient_source.title: Sugars, powdered (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FDC ID 19336)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry 19336 'Sugars, powdered' is the canonical approved-source match for icing/confectioners' sugar. Singapore HPB database values for energy (382 kcal), protein (0 g), fat (0 g), carbohydrate (100 g), sugar (100 g), sodium (0 mg), dietary fibre (0 g), potassium (2 mg), calcium (1 mg), and iron (0 mg) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy and are retained. USDA SR Legacy reports energy 389 kcal/100 g; HPB value of 382 kcal is used as the primary canonical value per source priority rules (HPB Singapore is primary regional source). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and micronutrients beyond potassium, calcium, and iron are confirmed negligible or not reported for pure sucrose powder.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — sucrose / table sugar
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: Icing sugar is finely milled sucrose with no additives. The GI of sucrose is consistently reported at approximately 65 (medium) in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International GI Tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (65 × 8.0) / 100 = 5.2. Serving size used: 8 g (1 tablespoon). The finer particle size of icing sugar versus granulated sugar may marginally accelerate absorption, but no separate GI value for icing sugar specifically is published; sucrose GI of 65 is the closest defensible approved-source value.

hypertension.key_factor: Pure refined sugar (100 g/100 g) with negligible potassium (2 mg/100 g) and zero dietary fibre; high added-sugar intake independently elevates blood pressure via insulin-mediated sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid using icing sugar as a sweetener and restrict use to minimal decorative dusting only, as habitual high added-sugar intake raises blood pressure independently of sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Icing sugar is 100 g sucrose per 100 g with a GI of 65 and zero protein, fat, or fibre to moderate glycaemic response; even small quantities cause rapid and significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid icing sugar entirely or limit use to trace decorative amounts, and consider sugar-free alternatives such as erythritol or stevia-based powders for baking applications.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added-sugar intake from pure sucrose (100 g/100 g) promotes hypertriglyceridaemia, hepatic de novo lipogenesis, and systemic inflammation, all of which are independent cardiovascular risk factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should minimise icing sugar consumption, as regular high added-sugar intake is associated with elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and increased cardiovascular event risk per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pure refined sucrose (100 g/100 g) is maximally pro-inflammatory; no anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols, fibre >3 g, antioxidants) are present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid icing sugar entirely, as pure refined sugar is one of the most pro-inflammatory dietary components and may exacerbate joint inflammation and disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Icing sugar, also known as powdered sugar or confectioners' sugar, is refined sucrose that has been finely milled into a smooth, white powder with a silky, dusty texture. It is produced by grinding granulated white sugar to a very fine particle size, sometimes with a small amount of anti-caking agent (such as cornstarch) added commercially, though pure icing sugar contains only sucrose. It dissolves almost instantly on contact with moisture, making it ideal for dusting over cakes, pastries, doughnuts, and baked goods, as well as for making smooth icings, frostings, glazes, and fondants. The taste is intensely sweet with no other flavour notes. Nutritionally, icing sugar is essentially pure carbohydrate (100 g/100 g), providing 382 kcal per 100 g with zero protein, zero fat, zero dietary fibre, and negligible micronutrients. Its glycemic index is approximately 65 (medium), and even small serving amounts (8 g per tablespoon) deliver a glycemic load of 5.2. Clinically, icing sugar is a high-concern food for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis due to its pure added-sugar composition, rapid glycaemic impact, and pro-inflammatory properties. It should be used only as a minimal decorative topping in clinical dietary contexts and avoided as a sweetener.
common_user_queries: Is icing sugar the same as powdered sugar?
How many calories in icing sugar?
Is icing sugar bad for diabetics?
Can I use icing sugar if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of icing sugar?
How much sugar is in icing sugar per 100g?

confidence_reason: Icing sugar is a simple, well-characterised processed food (pure milled sucrose) with consistent nutrient values across HPB Singapore and USDA SR Legacy. The HPB Singapore database is the primary approved regional source and reports values directly. Nutrient profile is internally consistent and matches the known composition of pure sucrose. GI is supported by the University of Sydney GI Database via the sucrose reference value.
notes: Zero-macro justification: protein_g is naturally zero — icing sugar is pure refined sucrose with no protein content by composition. fat_g is naturally zero — icing sugar contains no fat by formulation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 19336) and Singapore HPB database. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all confirmed zero for pure sucrose. iron_mg and cholesterol_mg are confirmed zero per HPB Singapore database. Negative sentinel values: vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1 as these micronutrients are not reported for pure sucrose/icing sugar in the HPB Singapore database or USDA SR Legacy; pure sucrose is not a meaningful source of these nutrients. water_g is reported as 0.2 g per USDA SR Legacy for powdered sugar. Ontology note: FoodEx2 code A000E is used as the closest defensible parent category anchor for sugar and sugar products; a dedicated icing sugar FoodEx2 code was not identified. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 (powdered sugar) is used as the exact match. No SNOMED CT code was identified for icing sugar. GI note: No published GI value exists specifically for icing sugar as a distinct product; the sucrose GI of 65 from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) is used as the closest defensible analogous value, as icing sugar is chemically identical to sucrose.
